Objective 41G

The Tongue — Muscles, Blood Supply, Innervation, Development & Histology

Oral-Cervical-Skull · Head & Neck Anatomy · Donor & Integrative Session

Contents

  1. Muscles of the Tongue
  2. Blood Supply
  3. Innervation
  4. Development of the Tongue
  5. Histology — Muscles & Taste Buds
  6. Practice MCQs
Part 1

Muscles of the Tongue

The tongue is a mobile muscular organ covered by mucosa. Its muscles are divided into two groups: extrinsic muscles (attach the tongue to external structures, alter position) and intrinsic muscles (entirely within the tongue, alter shape). Nearly all are innervated by CN XII — Hypoglossal, with the sole exception of palatoglossus, which is innervated by CN X — Vagus via the pharyngeal plexus.

Mnemonic All tongue muscles innervated by CN XII except palatoglossus (CN X). Remember: palatoglossus is functionally a palate muscle despite its tongue insertion.

Extrinsic Muscles

MuscleOriginInsertionActionInnervation
GenioglossusMental spine of mandibleInferior tongue & hyoid bodyProtrudes tongue; depresses center (fan-shaped)CN XII
HyoglossusGreater horn & body of hyoidLateral tongueDepresses & retracts tongueCN XII
StyloglossusStyloid process of temporal boneLateral tongue (interdigitates with hyoglossus)Retracts & elevates tongueCN XII
PalatoglossusPalatine aponeurosisLateral tongueElevates posterior tongue; closes oropharyngeal isthmusCN X (pharyngeal plexus)
Clinical Pearl — CN XII Lesion Damage to the hypoglossal nerve causes tongue deviation toward the side of the lesion (ipsilateral) on protrusion, because the contralateral genioglossus pushes the tongue to the weak side. "The tongue points to the problem."

Intrinsic Muscles

All intrinsic muscles are innervated by CN XII. They have no bony attachments and are named by their fiber orientation within the tongue.

MuscleFiber OrientationAction
Superior LongitudinalRuns anterior–posterior (superior layer)Shortens tongue; curls apex & lateral margins upward
Inferior LongitudinalRuns anterior–posterior (inferior layer)Shortens tongue; curls apex downward
TransverseRuns medial–lateralNarrows & elongates tongue
VerticalRuns superior–inferiorFlattens & broadens tongue
Histology note In histological sections, interlacing bundles of skeletal muscle are visible in three planes — horizontal (side-to-side), vertical (superior–inferior), and longitudinal (anterior–posterior) — giving the tongue its characteristic criss-cross appearance under the microscope.
Part 2

Blood Supply of the Tongue

The tongue receives its blood supply primarily from the lingual artery, a branch of the external carotid artery. Venous drainage is via the lingual vein into the internal jugular vein.

Arterial Supply

  • Lingual artery (branch of external carotid artery) — primary supply
  • Arises from the external carotid between the superior thyroid and facial arteries
  • Passes deep to the hyoglossus muscle
  • Branches: dorsal lingual arteries (posterior tongue & epiglottis), deep lingual artery (apex of tongue), sublingual artery (floor of mouth)
  • Minor contribution from tonsillar and ascending pharyngeal branches

Venous Drainage

  • Deep lingual vein — most prominent, visible on underside of tongue
  • Dorsal lingual veins — drain the dorsum
  • Both drain into the lingual vein → internal jugular vein
  • Sublingual vein drains floor of mouth and anastomoses with lingual veins
Clinical Relevance — Lingual Artery Ligation Because lingual arteries are essentially end-arteries with minimal midline anastomosis, unilateral ligation can reduce tongue hemorrhage. However, bilateral ligation risks tongue necrosis.
Part 3

Innervation of the Tongue

Tongue innervation is complex and must be separated into motor, general sensory, and special sensory (taste) components, and varies between the anterior 2/3 and the posterior 1/3.

ModalityAnterior 2/3Posterior 1/3
General Sensory (touch, pain, temp)Lingual nerve CN V3 (branch of mandibular)Glossopharyngeal nerve CN IX
Taste (Special Sensory)Chorda tympani CN VII (runs with lingual n.)Glossopharyngeal nerve CN IX
Motor (all tongue muscles)Hypoglossal nerve CN XII (except palatoglossus → CN X)
Note on Circumvallate Papillae Although the circumvallate papillae are located anterior to the sulcus terminalis, they are innervated for taste by CN IX (glossopharyngeal), consistent with their posterior 1/3 developmental origin. The skeletal muscle underlying them is still innervated by CN XII.
Additional Innervation — Root of Tongue The root of the tongue and the epiglottis are supplied by the internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (CN X — Vagus) for general sensation.

Summary Diagram (Innervation Overview)

Anterior 2/3 (Oral Tongue)

  • Lingual n. (V3) → touch, pain, temperature
  • Chorda tympani (VII) → taste & preganglionic parasympathetics to sublingual/submandibular glands
  • CN XII → motor

Posterior 1/3 (Pharyngeal Tongue)

  • CN IX (Glossopharyngeal) → both taste AND general sensory
  • CN XII → motor
  • CN X (internal laryngeal) → epiglottis / root
Part 4

Development of the Tongue

The tongue develops from pharyngeal arches 1–4 beginning at Week 4. Because different arches contribute to different regions, the innervation of each region follows its arch of origin — this explains the complex, multi-nerve innervation pattern of the adult tongue.

Pharyngeal Arch 1 (Mandibular)

Median lingual swelling — initially forms but is quickly overgrown by the lateral swellings.

Lateral lingual swellings (×2) — grow and fuse to overgrow the median swelling, forming the anterior 2/3 of the tongue.

→ Innervated by CN V3 (lingual n.) and CN VII (chorda tympani)

Pharyngeal Arch 2 (Hyoid)

Foramen cecum & Copula — contributes to the mucosal lining but is largely overgrown by arches 3 and 4. The foramen cecum marks the site of thyroid descent.

Pharyngeal Arch 3 (Glossopharyngeal)

Hypopharyngeal eminence (Rostral part) → overgrows arch 2 copula to form the posterior 1/3 of the tongue.

→ Innervated by CN IX (Glossopharyngeal)

Pharyngeal Arch 4 (Vagal)

Hypopharyngeal eminence (Caudal part) → forms the root of the tongue and a portion of the epiglottis.

→ Innervated by CN X (internal branch of superior laryngeal)

Sulcus Terminalis The boundary between the anterior 2/3 and posterior 1/3 is the V-shaped sulcus terminalis. Its apex is the foramen cecum, the remnant of the thyroglossal duct, where the thyroid primordium originated before descending into the neck.

Thyroid Gland Development (Related)

The thyroid primordium originates at the tongue floor (at the foramen cecum, between arches 1 and 2). It descends via the thyroglossal duct through the neck, passing ventral to the hyoid and laryngeal cartilages, reaching its final position by the 7th week when the duct obliterates. Failure of duct closure → thyroglossal duct cyst.

Part 5

Histology — Muscles & Taste Buds

The tongue is structurally described as "an epithelium-coated fibrous bag packed with skeletal muscle oriented in three planes." Understanding its histology requires knowledge of its epithelium, papillae, lamina propria, muscle architecture, and taste buds.

General Histological Architecture

Superior (Dorsal) Surface

  • Thick stratified squamous epithelium
  • Numerous papillae → roughened surface
  • Well-defined dense irregular connective tissue lamina propria
  • No true submucosa

Inferior (Ventral) Surface

  • Thinner non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium
  • Smooth — no papillae
  • Submucosa present, containing lingual salivary glands

Muscle Histology

Thick bundles of striated skeletal muscle are arranged in three orientations visible in cross section:

PlaneDirectionCorresponds to intrinsic muscle
Horizontal (transverse)Side to sideTransverse muscle
VerticalSuperior–inferiorVertical muscle
LongitudinalAnterior–posteriorSuperior & inferior longitudinal

These interlacing bundles give the tongue its characteristic criss-cross appearance in histological section.

Papillae — Types and Histology

Four types of papillae project from the dorsal surface. They are formed by a connective tissue core (lamina propria) covered by epithelium.

TypeShapeLocationTaste Buds?Key Features
FiliformThread-like, pointed tips; may have multiple epithelial tips per connective tissue papillaAnterior 2/3 (most numerous)NoKeratinized; provides friction for food manipulation; no taste function
FungiformMushroom-shaped: narrow stalk, broad round capScattered anterior 2/3, esp. tipRarely (occasional taste buds on lateral walls)Visible as red dots; far fewer than filiform
FoliateBlunt, rectangular leaf-like folds; can have spiky connective tissue apexLateral surface, posteriorYes — on lateral walls of papillae (especially in children)Degenerate with age; rudimentary in adult humans; serous minor salivary glands deep to them
Circumvallate (Vallate)Large circular; narrow stalk blossoming into globular head; surrounded by deep moatJust anterior to sulcus terminalis (~8–12 total)Yes — many, along walls of moatVon Ebner's glands at moat base (serous, flush moat); innervated by CN IX despite anterior location

Taste Bud Histology

Taste buds appear as oval/barrel-shaped structures embedded in the epithelium of the lateral walls of circumvallate and foliate papillae (and occasionally fungiform papillae). At the luminal apex is a small taste pore, through which luminal fluids interact with gustatory receptors.

Cell Types in a Taste Bud

  • Gustatory (Type II) neurons — elongated cells extending from basal lamina to taste pore; the primary receptor cells
  • Support (Sustentacular) cells — also elongated, basal lamina to pore; structurally support gustatory neurons; fewer in number
  • Basal cells — small, located at the basal lamina; stem cells that replenish gustatory and support cells (turnover ~10 days)

Innervation of Taste Buds

  • Gustatory neurons are contacted by afferent nerve endings from:
  • CN VII (chorda tympani) → anterior 2/3 taste buds
  • CN IX → circumvallate & foliate (posterior) taste buds
  • CN X → epiglottic & root taste buds
Von Ebner's Glands These are minor serous salivary glands located in the tongue musculature, with ducts opening at the base of the circumvallate papilla moat. They produce a serous (watery) fluid that continuously flushes the moat, removing food particles and allowing taste buds to detect new taste stimuli rapidly.

Practice MCQs

Click an option to check your answer, then reveal the explanation.

Q1 A patient presents with tongue deviation to the right on protrusion. Which nerve is most likely damaged, and on which side?

  • A Left CN XII (hypoglossal)
  • B Right CN XII (hypoglossal)
  • C Right CN X (vagus)
  • D Left CN V3 (lingual nerve)
Answer: B — Right CN XII. The genioglossus protrudes the tongue. With right CN XII damage, the right genioglossus is paralyzed; the intact left genioglossus pushes the tongue toward the paralyzed (right) side. "The tongue points to the problem."

Q2 A medical student touches the anterior 2/3 of the tongue and the patient feels it, but cannot taste anything on that side after a surgical procedure in the middle ear. Which structure was most likely damaged?

  • A Lingual nerve (CN V3)
  • B Chorda tympani (CN VII)
  • C Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)
  • D Hypoglossal nerve (CN XII)
Answer: B — Chorda tympani. General sensation (touch) in the anterior 2/3 is carried by the lingual nerve (V3) — still intact (patient feels touch). Taste in the anterior 2/3 is carried by the chorda tympani (CN VII branch), which traverses the middle ear and is vulnerable to injury during middle ear surgery. Loss of taste with preserved touch = chorda tympani damage.

Q3 Which papillae type is keratinized, provides mechanical friction for food manipulation, and contains NO taste buds?

  • A Filiform
  • B Fungiform
  • C Foliate
  • D Circumvallate
Answer: A — Filiform papillae. These are the most numerous papillae, thread-like, keratinized, and lack taste buds entirely. They serve a mechanical rather than gustatory function. Fungiform occasionally have taste buds; foliate and circumvallate papillae are well-supplied with taste buds.

Q4 During embryological development, the anterior 2/3 of the tongue forms from which pharyngeal arch structure?

  • A Median lingual swelling (Arch 1)
  • B Lateral lingual swellings (Arch 1)
  • C Copula (Arch 2)
  • D Hypopharyngeal eminence, rostral portion (Arch 3)
Answer: B — Lateral lingual swellings (Arch 1). The lateral lingual swellings (paired) originate from Arch 1 and overgrow the unpaired median lingual swelling to form the anterior 2/3. The median swelling is vestigial. The posterior 1/3 arises from the rostral hypopharyngeal eminence of Arch 3.

Q5 Von Ebner's glands are serous glands that drain into the base of the moat surrounding which papillae type? What is their function?

  • A Foliate — to lubricate lateral tongue wall
  • B Filiform — to moisten the anterior tongue
  • C Circumvallate — to flush the moat and renew taste stimuli
  • D Fungiform — to deliver saliva to the tongue tip
Answer: C — Circumvallate. Von Ebner's glands are serous minor salivary glands whose ducts open at the bottom of the moat surrounding circumvallate papillae. They produce a thin serous secretion that continuously washes out the moat, removing old taste molecules so that taste buds can detect fresh stimuli efficiently.

Q6 Which muscle of the tongue is innervated by the vagus nerve (CN X) rather than the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII)?

  • A Genioglossus
  • B Hyoglossus
  • C Styloglossus
  • D Palatoglossus
Answer: D — Palatoglossus. Although the palatoglossus inserts on the tongue, it is embryologically and functionally a palate muscle. It is innervated by the pharyngeal plexus via CN X (vagus), not CN XII. All other tongue muscles (intrinsic and extrinsic) are innervated by CN XII.

Q7 A histology slide shows oval structures embedded in the lateral walls of papillae with cells extending from a basal lamina to a luminal pore. Small stem cells are visible at the base. Which cell types are being described in order (from most to least numerous)?

  • A Support cells → gustatory neurons → basal cells
  • B Gustatory neurons → support cells → basal cells
  • C Basal cells → gustatory neurons → support cells
  • D Goblet cells → gustatory neurons → basal cells
Answer: B — Gustatory neurons → support cells → basal cells. Each taste bud contains three cell types: (1) Gustatory neurons — the most numerous, elongated, extending to the taste pore, they are the primary sensory receptor cells contacted by afferent nerve endings. (2) Support (sustentacular) cells — also elongated, fewer in number, provide structural support. (3) Basal cells — small stem cells at the base that replenish both cell types with a turnover of approximately 10 days. Goblet cells are not found in taste buds.
